meta.title: Quiz 3 — Node.js, Express & Server-Side
meta.description: Node.js runtime, Express middleware, routing, templating, and server-side concepts.

questions.questionMd: When using `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia()` with a Promise, how is the camera stream accessed?

options.label: A
options.text: By reading the return value of the function directly

options.label: B
options.text: By listening for a DOM event fired when the camera opens

options.label: C
options.text: By passing an onSuccess callback as the second argument

options.label: D
options.text: By attaching a .then() handler that receives the stream as its argument

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, what does it mean to "deserialize" a string?

options.label: A
options.text: To encode a string using HTML entities

options.label: B
options.text: To split a string into an array using a separator

options.label: C
options.text: To turn a serialized string back into a JavaScript object

options.label: D
options.text: To convert a JavaScript object into a JSON string

questions.explanationMd: Deserialization is rebuilding a native object structure from a serialized format.

questions.questionMd: Why does calling `fetch()` on a local file opened via `file://` protocol result in an error?

options.label: A
options.text: Loading files via the `file://` protocol is disallowed by the browser (CORS).

options.label: B
options.text: JavaScript blocks file IO on the main thread.

options.label: C
options.text: The API only supports HTML files, not raw files.

options.label: D
options.text: The OS blocks file reading from HTML index pages.

questions.explanationMd: Browsers consider local files to be separate origins. Accessing them via fetch triggers Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) errors to protect local file systems.

questions.questionMd: Why is storing data as JSON preferable to storing it as plain text when using `fetch()`?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON is the only format the Fetch API is able to read

options.label: B
options.text: JSON files load faster because they are compressed by the browser

options.label: C
options.text: JSON eliminates the need to manually parse field separators when deserializing structured data

options.label: D
options.text: Plain text requires XMLHttpRequest while JSON works with fetch

questions.explanationMd: JSON provides structured key-value pairs inherently, saving developers from manually splitting plain text strings.

questions.questionMd: Which function would you use to convert a JavaScript object into a JSON-formatted string?

options.label: A
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: B
options.text: JSON.stringify()

options.label: C
options.text: JSON.encode()

options.label: D
options.text: JSON.deserialize()

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.stringify()` converts an object to a string format suitable for transmission.

questions.questionMd: What is the key difference between `navigator.getUserMedia` and `navigator.mediaDevices.getUserMedia`?

options.label: A
options.text: The newer version requires HTTPS while the deprecated version does not

options.label: B
options.text: The deprecated version uses Promises; the newer version uses callbacks

options.label: C
options.text: Only the deprecated version supports video streaming

options.label: D
options.text: The newer version returns a Promise, the deprecated version uses callbacks

questions.explanationMd: Modern web standard APIs frequently utilize Promises for cleaner async flows compared to older callback methods.

questions.questionMd: What does JSON stand for?

options.label: A
options.text: Java Serialized Object Network

options.label: B
options.text: JavaScript Syntax Object Notation

options.label: C
options.text: JavaScript Object Notation

options.label: D
options.text: JavaScript Ordered Node

questions.explanationMd: JSON stands for JavaScript Object Notation.

questions.questionMd: Which method on a fetch `response` object provides built-in support for parsing JSON directly?

options.label: A
options.text: response.text()

options.label: B
options.text: response.json()

options.label: C
options.text: response.parse()

options.label: D
options.text: response.decode()

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly rewrites `fetch('f.txt').then(onResponse)` using an intermediate variable?

options.label: A
options.text: let p = onResponse; p = fetch('f.txt');

options.label: B
options.text: const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.resolve(onResponse);

options.label: C
options.text: const p = new Promise('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

options.label: D
options.text: const p = fetch('f.txt'); p.then(onResponse);

questions.explanationMd: Storing the promise returned by `fetch()` into a variable `p` allows you to attach handlers to it later using `p.then()`.

questions.questionMd: In the completed fetch example, which JavaScript method is used to break the fetched text into individual image URLs?

options.label: A
options.text: text.join(' ')

options.label: B
options.text: text.replace(' ', ',')

options.label: C
options.text: text.slice(' ')

options.label: D
options.text: text.split(' ')

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: When chaining `.then()` calls on a fetch Promise, what does the second `.then()` receive as its argument?

options.label: A
options.text: The value returned or resolved by the first .then() handler

options.label: B
options.text: The HTTP status code from the first .then()

options.label: C
options.text: The original fetch response object

options.label: D
options.text: A new fetch Promise targeting the same resource

questions.explanationMd: Promises pipe their resolution values forward; the return value of one `.then()` becomes the input argument to the next.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.status` contain when used inside a fetch success handler?

options.label: A
options.text: A boolean indicating whether the request succeeded

options.label: B
options.text: The HTTP status code of the request, such as 200

options.label: C
options.text: The text content of the fetched resource

options.label: D
options.text: The name of the file that was fetched

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: How does `response.text()` read the response data according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Synchronously, by returning the string directly

options.label: B
options.text: Asynchronously, by returning a Promise that resolves with the response string

options.label: C
options.text: By dispatching a DOM event when reading is complete

options.label: D
options.text: By writing the result into a global variable

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, which statement best captures the relationship between using and defining Promises?

options.label: A
options.text: Promises are easier to define than to use

options.label: B
options.text: Promises must be fully understood internally before they can be used

options.label: C
options.text: Promises are easier to use than to define

options.label: D
options.text: Promises are equally straightforward to both define and use

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, the Fetch API is made up of how many functions?

options.label: A
options.text: One

options.label: B
options.text: Two

options.label: C
options.text: Four

options.label: D
options.text: Three

questions.explanationMd: The primary interaction surface of the Fetch API is a single function: `fetch()`.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "fulfilled" state of a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: The Promise is waiting for a .then() handler to be attached

options.label: B
options.text: The operation encountered a network error

options.label: C
options.text: The Promise has been created but not yet started

options.label: D
options.text: The asynchronous operation completed successfully

questions.explanationMd: A 'fulfilled' promise means the underlying asynchronous operation was successful and a value was returned.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary purpose of the Fetch API in a web browser?

options.label: A
options.text: To serialize JavaScript objects into strings

options.label: B
options.text: To define callback functions for user interface events

options.label: C
options.text: To manipulate the DOM after a page has loaded

options.label: D
options.text: To load external resources such as text and JSON asynchronously

questions.explanationMd: Fetch is the modern API for making HTTP requests in browsers, allowing for network resource retrieval.

questions.questionMd: In a chained Promise pattern, what must `onResponse` return for the next `.then()` in the chain to receive the text data?

options.label: A
options.text: The response object itself

options.label: B
options.text: The Promise returned by response.text()

options.label: C
options.text: A JSON object created with JSON.parse()

options.label: D
options.text: A resolved string from response.status

questions.explanationMd: To chain asynchronous operations, returning the promise generated by `response.text()` ensures the next `.then()` waits for the text payload.

questions.questionMd: What is the correct syntax for using the real Fetch API with success and error handlers?

options.label: A
options.text: fetch('file.txt', onSuccess, onError);

options.label: B
options.text: fetch('file.txt').catch(onSuccess).then(onError);

options.label: C
options.text: fetch('file.txt').then(onSuccess, onError);

options.label: D
options.text: fetch('file.txt', { success: onSuccess, error: onError });

questions.explanationMd: A standard Promise `.then()` method accepts up to two arguments: a fulfillment handler and a rejection handler.

questions.questionMd: What does `JSON.parse()` accept as its argument?

options.label: A
options.text: A JavaScript object to be serialized

options.label: B
options.text: A JSON-formatted string

options.label: C
options.text: A Promise returned by fetch()

options.label: D
options.text: A fetch response object

questions.explanationMd: `JSON.parse()` parses a string that conforms to the JSON specification, producing a JavaScript value or object described by the string.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following correctly describes the old API that `fetch()` replaced for loading browser resources?

options.label: A
options.text: XMLHttpRequest (XHR)

options.label: B
options.text: JSON.parse()

options.label: C
options.text: loadFromFile()

options.label: D
options.text: navigator.mediaDevices

questions.explanationMd: Fetch is a modern, Promise-based improvement over the older, callback-based XMLHttpRequest (XHR) API.

questions.questionMd: Who created JSON according to the lecture?

options.label: A
options.text: Ryan Dahl

options.label: B
options.text: Douglas Crockford

options.label: C
options.text: Brendan Eich

options.label: D
options.text: Tim Berners-Lee

questions.explanationMd: Douglas Crockford popularized and specified the JSON data format.

questions.questionMd: What does `response.text()` return?

options.label: A
options.text: The raw bytes of the response body as a string immediately

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the response body as a string

options.label: C
options.text: A JSON object parsed from the response body

options.label: D
options.text: The HTTP status code as a text string

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What does the `fetch()` function return?

options.label: A
options.text: A JSON-parsed JavaScript object

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise

options.label: C
options.text: An XMLHttpRequest object

options.label: D
options.text: The raw text content of the requested resource

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: A Promise object can be in one of three states. Which of the following is NOT one of those states?

options.label: A
options.text: Pending

options.label: B
options.text: Resolved

options.label: C
options.text: Rejected

options.label: D
options.text: Fulfilled

questions.explanationMd: The states are Pending, Fulfilled, and Rejected. 'Resolved' describes the outcome, but the strict state name is Fulfilled.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following best describes the "pending" state of a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: The state where then() has been called but not yet attached

options.label: B
options.text: The initial state where the operation has not yet fulfilled or rejected

options.label: C
options.text: The state where the error handler has been triggered

options.label: D
options.text: The state where the operation completed but returned no data

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: In the completed fetch example, how is each image added to the web page after its URL is extracted?

options.label: A
options.text: A new element is created and appended to document.body

options.label: B
options.text: The image URL is written directly into the HTML using innerHTML

options.label: C
options.text: The URL is passed to a separate fetch call to load the image data

options.label: D
options.text: A CSS background property is applied to an existing element

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What type of value does `JSON.stringify(object)` return?

options.label: A
options.text: A boolean indicating whether serialization succeeded

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the serialized data

options.label: C
options.text: A new JavaScript object with the same properties

options.label: D
options.text: A string representing the object in JSON format

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: How can a developer serve local files over HTTP so that `fetch()` works correctly?

options.label: A
options.text: Use the file:// protocol with a special fetch configuration option

options.label: B
options.text: Run a local HTTP server, such as with python -m http.server

options.label: C
options.text: Enable CORS headers directly in the HTML file

options.label: D
options.text: Switch from fetch() to XMLHttpRequest for local files

questions.explanationMd: No explanation provided.

questions.questionMd: What is the primary difference between a URL that points to a static file and a URL that represents a REST API endpoint?

options.label: A
options.text: A static file URL always uses HTTPS, while a REST API does not.

options.label: B
options.text: A REST API endpoint causes the server to dynamically generate a response, whereas a static file URL causes the server to return a stored file.

options.label: C
options.text: A REST API returns HTML files only.

options.label: D
options.text: A static file requires form submission.

questions.explanationMd: Static files are merely retrieved from the server's disk, while REST endpoints trigger code execution to build dynamic responses (often in JSON).

questions.questionMd: What happens when the `await` keyword is used before a Promise?

options.label: A
options.text: It resolves instantly.

options.label: B
options.text: It throws a syntax error.

options.label: C
options.text: It pauses the execution of the async function until the Promise resolves.

options.label: D
options.text: It bypasses the `.catch` block.

questions.explanationMd: `await` is a mechanism inside `async` functions to wait for a Promise to settle (resolve or reject) before moving to the next line of code.

questions.questionMd: Which of the following is TRUE about the `status` property of a `Response` object?

options.label: A
options.text: It contains the routing configuration.

options.label: B
options.text: It contains the numeric HTTP status code (e.g., 200, 404).

options.label: C
options.text: It tells the user the state of their internet connection.

options.label: D
options.text: It logs script errors inside the browser console.

questions.explanationMd: The `Response.status` read-only property contains the status code of the response (e.g., 200 for a success).

questions.questionMd: When is the `.catch()` block executed in a Promise chain?

options.label: A
options.text: Always as the final step.

options.label: B
options.text: Only when specifically invoked.

options.label: C
options.text: When any Promise in the chain is rejected or an error is thrown.

options.label: D
options.text: If a user cancels the HTTP request.

questions.explanationMd: `.catch()` handles rejections that bubble up from anywhere in the preceding `.then()` chain.

questions.questionMd: What does the `Response.text()` method return when called inside a fetch success handler?

options.label: A
options.text: The raw bytes of the response body

options.label: B
options.text: A Promise that resolves with the response body as a string.

options.label: C
options.text: The HTML source code of the requesting page

options.label: D
options.text: The text representation of the loaded header

questions.explanationMd: Like `.json()`, `.text()` returns a Promise because parsing the body stream is an asynchronous operation.

questions.questionMd: How does the browser know what kind of data is in an HTTP response body?

options.label: A
options.text: By analyzing the body content with an algorithm

options.label: B
options.text: By checking the response body using a regular expression

options.label: C
options.text: By reading the file extension of the URL

options.label: D
options.text: By reading the `Content-Type` header in the response.

questions.explanationMd: The `Content-Type` HTTP header indicates the media type (MIME type) of the resource, such as `application/json` or `text/html`.

questions.questionMd: Which method of the Fetch API `Response` object is used to parse a JSON body?

options.label: A
options.text: .text()

options.label: B
options.text: .decode()

options.label: C
options.text: .json()

options.label: D
options.text: .parse()

questions.explanationMd: The `.json()` method of the Response interface reads the body stream and parses it as JSON.

questions.questionMd: What does `console.log()` return?

options.label: A
options.text: A string of the printed values

options.label: B
options.text: A Boolean value

options.label: C
options.text: undefined

options.label: D
options.text: The DOM node where the console output appears

questions.explanationMd: `console.log()` is a side-effect function that outputs text to the console, and inherently returns `undefined`.

questions.questionMd: According to the lecture, an API is a set of functions that allows what?

options.label: A
options.text: The OS to run multiple GUI apps

options.label: B
options.text: Servers to bypass security walls

options.label: C
options.text: Software applications to communicate with each other.

options.label: D
options.text: System synthesizers to access network memory

questions.explanationMd: An Application Programming Interface (API) provides a defined set of rules and protocols for building and interacting with software applications.

questions.questionMd: In the context of HTTP status codes, which of the following signifies "Not Found"?
questions.explanationMd: The HTTP 404 Not Found response code indicates that the server cannot find the requested resource.

questions.questionMd: Why are local files typically served over HTTP during development?

options.label: A
options.text: To bypass CORS restrictions that prevent local file access.

options.label: B
options.text: Because JSON parsing only works via HTTP

options.label: C
options.text: To make the application available globally on the web

options.label: D
options.text: Because the browser engine requires an active proxy

questions.explanationMd: Running a local HTTP server emulates a real web server environment, circumventing the browser's strict security protocols around `file://` URLs.
